20260615---cpap-pi---won-pcb-design-revi.title: 20260615 - CPAP-PI - Won PCB design review
20260615---cpap-pi---won-pcb-design-revi.content:
20260615 - CPAP-PI - Won PCB design review
Context
CPAP-PI project PCB design. Won was using Claude Code to generate PCB schematic design and layout — not a good approach. Xinye reviewed and guided fixes.
Issues Found

All GND connected via traces instead of a dedicated GND plane
Bottom layers completely empty on both boards (control board + mask flex board)
Mask flex board had insufficient area to route all lines → had to go to 6 layers

Fixes Applied

Adjusted component positions and orientations
Added dedicated GND layers for both boards

Outcome

Won generated Gerber files and submitted to PCBWAY for audit at ~9:30 PM
Deadline: Order Monday — met after Xinye’s guidance/kaMoamoa/cpap-pi/pcb-design


Related

KaMoamoa MOC > CPAP-PI

20260701---cpap-pi---board-assembly,-flu.filePath: 20260701 - CPAP-PI - Board assembly, flux and BATT-GND short.md
20260701---cpap-pi---board-assembly,-flu.title: 20260701 - CPAP-PI - Board assembly, flux and BATT-GND short
20260701---cpap-pi---board-assembly,-flu.content:
20260701 - CPAP-PI - Board assembly, flux and BATT-GND short
Night assembly session on two CPAP-PI control boards Won brought over — both lacked MCU modules (fine-pitch; earlier attempts failed because the module snapped back off its pads when nudged, pulled by the surface tension of the molten solder).
Root cause: wrong flux
We only had solid flux, which is unusable for fine-pitch SMD — there isn’t enough activity for the module to self-align. Need liquid / gel flux for QFN-style modules. (Won brought a flux syringe at 6pm.)
Assembly

Soldered the MCU modules with hot air.
Repurposed SWD connectors cannibalized from existing boards (forgot to order new ones — order spares).

Board #1 — works after reflow
Didn’t work initially; suspected a poor MCU solder joint. Bumped hot air to 400 °C for ~2 min to reflow the joints in place (without removing the module). After reflow, everything worked. ✅
Board #2 — abandoned (mystery short)

BATT–GND short detected. Systematically removed BATT-pin components and traced it to a regulator + two male headers.
After removing those the first short cleared, but the DC supply still current-saturated → another short remains. **Gave up on board 2 — not worth more hours.



                  
                  Reusable bench lessons 
                  
                


Solid flux is unusable for fine-pitch QFN modules — need liquid/gel flux so surface tension self-aligns the part.
Hot-air reflow trick: 400 °C for ~2 min revives a board with stubborn MCU joints without pulling the module.
Order spare SWD connectors — don’t rely on cannibalizing existing boards.



Related

KaMoamoa MOC > CPAP-PI
20260701 - CPAP-PI - Power design flaw & firmware fix — firmware work from the same night
Knowledge MOC > Soldering — flux / reflow technique

20260701---cpap-pi---power-design-flaw--.filePath: 20260701 - CPAP-PI - Power design flaw & firmware fix.md
20260701---cpap-pi---power-design-flaw--.title: 20260701 - CPAP-PI - Power design flaw & firmware fix
20260701---cpap-pi---power-design-flaw--.content:
Power Design Flaw & Resolution — CPAP-PI Control Board
The Flaw
The nRF52840 module on the CPAP-PI board is operated in high-voltage mode, where the internal DC/DC regulator supplies the core from VDDH. However, VDDH is fed from the regulated 3.3 V rail instead of the raw battery (~3.7 V) it’s designed for.
This has a critical consequence: in high-voltage mode, the GPIO voltage is set by a non-volatile (NV) register that defaults to 1.8 V and is wiped on every full-erase flash. When erased:

GPIO sits at 1.8 V while the board’s external pull-ups and connected peripherals run at 3.3 V — out of spec
The SPI pressure sensors don’t get enough drive voltage

This makes the board non-functional after a routine full-erase flash until the register is manually reprogrammed.
Firmware Fix
Added an early boot hook that runs before any peripheral initialization:

Check if the GPIO voltage NV setting is at its 1.8 V default
If so, reprogram it to 3.3 V and trigger a system reset
On the second boot (after reset), the setting persists and the hook does nothing

Self-heals automatically after every full-erase flash. No effect on normal boots or incremental flashes.
Verification

GPIO voltage setting reads back 3.3 V ✅
I2C mux responds ✅
Analog reference measures designed 0.30 V ✅

Flashing Note
Routine flashing without the --erase (full-erase) option avoids even the brief first-boot exposure.
Physical Fix (Documented, Not Implemented)
The current board could be reworked with a single jumper:

Connect VDD directly to 3.3 V
Keep VDDH connected as-is

This would eliminate the NV register dependency entirely. On a future PCB spin, the correct fix is powering VDDH from the battery with the internal regulator producing 3.3 V.
Decision: Skipping the jumper rework — the firmware fix is clean and reliable. Recorded here for the next spin.
Related

KaMoamoa MOC > CPAP-PI — parent project MOC
Caterpillar — reference project for nRF Connect SDK custom board setup

20260702---cpap-pi---sensor-stack-bringu.filePath: 20260702 - CPAP-PI - Sensor stack bringup and binary data pipeline.md
20260702---cpap-pi---sensor-stack-bringu.title: 20260702 - CPAP-PI - Sensor stack bringup and binary data pipeline
20260702---cpap-pi---sensor-stack-bringu.content:
20260702 - CPAP-PI - Sensor stack bringup and binary data pipeline
Full bringup of the CPAP-PI sensor stack and the firmware ↔ web-portal data pipeline. End state: the whole pipeline verified live — 2× PPG + 3× FSR channels + 5× barometers + SHT40 streaming at target rates to the portal over both BLE and wired RTT.
Sensor stack (hardware-verified over debug console)

Devicetree-native topology using in-tree drivers (mux, PPG, SHT40) plus a custom MS5611 driver with correct compensation math.
Boot diagnostics scan every bus and report per-sensor health each boot.
Hardware debug:

Mask FPC seating — reversed pinout confirmed.
Missing 1.8 V rail was killing all PPGs; solder rework (remove/reinstall the FPC connector on the control board) fixed 2 of 3.
PPG red/IR ADC saturation on skin contact (LED currents at max default) — retuned; pulse waveforms now visible.


Two devices still dead (PPG1, baro1 — adjacent connector pins or chip joints, deferred); they rejoin automatically at boot once fixed.
Timing bug: the sampling loop’s sleep-after-work stretched the 10 ms tick to ~14 ms; switching to absolute-deadline scheduling restored true 100 Hz.

Data pipeline (firmware ↔ web portal)

Replaced JSON-over-BLE with a compact binary protocol over Nordic UART Service (NUS): 25 frames/s, batching 4 ticks each (3× PPG R/IR/G, 3× FSR + reference, 6× barometers), plus a 1 Hz status frame (env, resistances, rates, health masks). ~5.7 kB/s, within Web Bluetooth limits.
Portal rewritten to parse binary frames: multi-sensor charts, overlay-vs-per-signal split-view toggle (full-width plots), extended CSV export; dead sensors auto-hidden via health masks.
Wired path via an RTT bridge script → local WebSocket; portal gained an “RTT (wired)” mode replacing Web Serial. Lossless — ground truth vs BLE.
Raised barometers from 25 Hz to 100 Hz (pipelined conversions, no resolution loss); frame format updated end-to-end and verified with distinct per-sample readings.

State & open items
Everything committed and pushed (firmware + portal repos). Open items: resolder PPG1/baro1, attach/verify FSR tails, next-spin PCB power fix.
Related

KaMoamoa MOC > CPAP-PI
20260701 - CPAP-PI - Power design flaw & firmware fix
20260701 - CPAP-PI - Board assembly, flux and BATT-GND short

20260702---cpap-pi---won-setup-and-two-t.filePath: 20260702 - CPAP-PI - Won setup and two-track branching.md
20260702---cpap-pi---won-setup-and-two-t.title: 20260702 - CPAP-PI - Won setup and two-track branching
20260702---cpap-pi---won-setup-and-two-t.content:
20260702 - CPAP-PI - Won setup and two-track branching
Set up the CPAP-PI firmware and portal on Won’s computer, and settled a two-track development structure so the eval rig and the full board can progress in parallel. Won had enhanced the older portal version with his own features; those were integrated into the new portal version (using Claude on his machine).
Two parallel tracks























TrackHardwarePortalFirmware branchEval systemDK + breakout boards + breadboard + jumpersold enhanced portalevalFull boardthe CPAP-PI PCBfull portal (Won’s enhancement branch)main
The eval system is for mechanical-integration testing; the full board runs the production stack.
Branches

Firmware: main (full board), eval (eval system).
Portal: Won’s enhancement branch (full portal); the old enhanced portal (eval) lives on disk.

Related

KaMoamoa MOC > CPAP-PI

index.title: CPAP-PI — Dev Notes
index.content:
CPAP-PI is a small BLE multi-sensor data-acquisition board (with Won on
hardware): an nRF52840 streaming PPG, FSR, MS5611 barometers, and
an SHT40 temp/humidity sensor to a web portal over a compact binary protocol
(BLE and wired RTT).
Firmware: CPAP_PI_firmware.
This is a running development log — newest first.
Jul 2026 — bringup & pipeline

20260702 - CPAP-PI - Sensor stack bringup and binary data pipeline — full sensor-stack bringup and the firmware↔portal binary pipeline (BLE + wired RTT), verified live.
20260701 - CPAP-PI - Power design flaw & firmware fix — nRF52840 GPIO voltage defaults to 1.8 V after a full-erase flash; a self-healing early-boot fix reprograms it to 3.3 V.
20260701 - CPAP-PI - Board assembly, flux and BATT-GND short — hand-assembly of two boards, the SMD-flux lesson, and a BATT–GND short hunt.

Jun 2026 — design & first board

20260622 - CPAP-PI - BOM generation and VDD circuit fix — BOM generation and a VDD circuit fix.
20260615 - CPAP-PI - Won PCB design review — early PCB design review with Won.
